So let's review one last time here, these eight tonal patterns. Number one is scarcity, that people want more of what there's less than. And we do that by lowering our voice in this sort of stressed urgency. Remember it's scarcity slash urgency. Second tonality is your reasonable man tone. I'm reasonable, you're reasonable, let's do it. So you know, got a minute? Fair enough? Sound fair enough? Reasonable man tone versus sound fair enough? We're like, oh my god, no it doesn't sound fair, right? So it's your reasonable man tone. Number three is absolute certainty. This thing really does make sense. It comes from here, it's that power inside, it's that force. Not screamed, but absolute certainty. Then you have the I care tone, which is again, I really want it to go hand in hand. Next you have using a question when there should be a declarative. So that's where I start off and say, you know, Jordan Belfort calling from Sales Acute in Australia, and they're like, who, what? When you paralyze their internal dialogue and you move forward then and they're still listening and in state versus judging you and hanging up on you, right? Next we have our series of three up tones where we hook in three up tones together and we create this sort of picture like where they just want to agree with us. You're like, right, right, right, the unspoken words like Jordan Belfort from Sales Acute, you know, right, right, right, and they're like, aha, and they just agree with you so watch what happens in that situation where they would actually not really know who you are but just say, aha, yeah, I heard of you simply because they want to agree because of the tonality. Next we have our presupposing tone, which is where we talk about something in such powerful terms that it's not even a question of whether or not you'll make money here. It's not a question whether it's good or bad. We already know that. Now you can't say to someone, John, it's not a question of whether or not you'll like this. We all know you're going to like that. If you say, I'll say, no, I don't. You couldn't say it to the guy. So instead you say, it's like, not a question of whether or not it works. But the tonality, he's like, and you move past it and all of a sudden he's like, aha, and you're transitioning to something else and bam, you've established that yes, he's on the same page with you. And then lastly, the I really want to know tone, which is hand to hand with I care. You know, how you doing today? I really want to know that unspoken phrase. I really want to know that I care about you. So these tonalities when used at the right times and done exactly in the right fashion using the right pitch is going to allow you to move your clients emotionally to a spot of absolute certainty. And they won't really even know why. It's the words that will give them the logic. Yeah, A plus B equals C, one plus one is two. Yeah, that's the words, that's logic. But it's that other part, that tonal, that emotional move that's going to have into that spot of, yeah, just let's do it. And you get those two elements, logic and emotion lined up at the highest level. That's when people start to buy. You might say, well, people really see through the stuff. Guess what? They don't. They almost can't. And just, I'm not saying that if you just use these tonalities, you're going to close the sale automatically. What I'm saying is if you don't use them, you're going to blow a sale. And if you use these tonalities again and again and again to keep that person's unconscious dialogue right in lockstep with yours along with the right words and you do things in the right order, the right syntax, then wow, this collectively, this tonality just is just unbelievably powerful, unbelievably effective. And it's almost like it's the enhancer to everything else that you do. It just kicks it up a notch. So what I do when I write a script for myself, in any situation, actually, I first will think, where are the tonal changes? Where should I raise my voice? Where should I lower my voice? Where should I speed up? Where should I slow down? Very often in scripts, when I see a real novice salesperson, they don't understand that, you know, human speech is not said, hello, Victoria, how are you today? The enemy of all influence with tonality is what's called constancy, being constant. The same thing again and again and again. People will just tune out.
